insights: No identical expense claims exist across all 500 records when matching on user, amount, category, and short_description — indicating zero instances of exact repetition that could suggest policy misunderstanding or fraud.

No duplicate (user, amount) pairs exist in the dataset, confirming that no individual submitted the same dollar amount more than once — eliminating a core behavioral signal of repetitive claiming behavior.

Only three near-duplicate expense amounts (within $100 tolerance) were found across the entire dataset — involving benjamin60, jennifer15, and arthurhernandez — and these lack supporting alignment in category or description, rendering them statistically negligible and contextually unactionable.

The absence of repeated claims under both strict and relaxed matching criteria indicates that neither systemic policy confusion nor coordinated fraudulent repetition is present in this expense population.
Resource-intensive investigation into root causes, approval patterns, or temporal clustering of duplicates is unwarranted, as the foundational premise of repetition is not substantiated by the data.
summary: The analysis confirms that no repeated identical expense claims exist in the dataset — a finding consistent across exact matching on (user, amount, category, short_description) and relaxed matching on (user, amount ±$100). With zero exact duplicates and only three isolated near-duplicates lacking contextual consistency, there is no evidence to support either fraudulent intent or widespread policy misinterpretation. This null result eliminates the need for deeper forensic review of claim timing, approver behavior, or category-specific anomalies. The next appropriate step is formal closure of the duplicate-claim investigation, with continued monitoring reserved only for future datasets showing deviation from this clean pattern.
